title: Why invalidation is hard
description: TTL, explicit invalidation, and versioned keys trade staleness, coupling, and race-safety differently.
body: Use when deciding how cached entries become stale. TTL expiry is simple with bounded staleness and no write coupling; explicit invalidation gives low staleness but must catch every write path; versioned keys bump a version on write so old keys are abandoned, sidestepping races. Do not rely on explicit invalidation alone — a single missed write path leaves a permanently stale entry. Actions: combine explicit invalidation with a backstop TTL. Validation signals include reads reflecting writes within the bound. Risk boundary: derived and aggregate keys are the ones most often forgotten when invalidating.
